subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — Constitutional Checks and Balances vs. Social Movements in Representing the People's Will
stimulus: An important feature of a democratic republic is that it represents the will of the people. Take a position on whether constitutional checks and balances or citizen participation in social movements is more effective in ensuring the people's will is represented.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. (Use at least one piece of evidence from one of the following foundational documents: Article I of the United States Constitution, The Federalist 10, "Letter from a Birmingham Jail".)
rubric_criteria: 1 pt (Row A — Thesis/Claim): Responds to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis establishing a line of reasoning about whether constitutional checks and balances or social movements are more effective in representing the people's will.

Examples that earn the point: 'Checks and balances are more effective in ensuring the people's will is represented because they guarantee that parts of the government that defy the people will be counteracted or removed entirely.' OR 'Social movements are more effective because they act independently of the government and can force the government to respond to the will of the people.'
parts.scoring_notes: Must take a clear position. Generic statements about checks and balances being a feature of the Constitution do not earn the point.

parts.prompt: Support your claim with at least TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence. One piece of evidence must come from one of the foundational documents listed. A second piece of evidence can come from any other foundational document or from course concepts.
rubric_criteria: 0 pts: No accurate evidence, or evidence not relevant to the topic.
1 pt (Row B — 1 point): One piece of evidence relevant to the topic.
2 pts (Row B — 2 points): One piece of specific and relevant evidence supports the claim (requires Row A).
3 pts (Row B — 3 points): Two pieces of specific and relevant evidence, one from a listed document (Article I, Federalist 10, or Letter from Birmingham Jail) (requires Row A).

Examples of acceptable evidence: 'Article I gives Congress the power to override presidential vetoes, ensuring checks on executive overreach.' 'Federalist 10 argues that competing factions in a republic require institutional mechanisms to prevent any single faction from dominating.' 'King's Letter from Birmingham Jail argues that direct action and civil disobedience are necessary when legal channels fail to represent marginalized groups.' 'The Civil Rights Act of 1964 was passed partly in response to social movement pressure.' 'Senate confirmation of presidential appointments is a constitutional check.'
parts.scoring_notes: To earn 2 or 3 points, must have earned Row A. To earn 3 points, one piece of evidence must come from Article I, Federalist 10, or Letter from Birmingham Jail.

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt (Row C — Reasoning): Explains the relationship between evidence and the claim.

Examples that earn the point: 'Federalist 10 argues that institutional mechanisms are necessary to mediate between competing factions, suggesting that checks and balances are a more reliable and durable means of representing the people's will than the unpredictable nature of social movements.' 'King's Letter demonstrates that social movements become necessary when constitutional mechanisms fail to represent marginalized groups, showing that social movements can reach citizens that formal institutions overlook.'
parts.scoring_notes: Must have earned Row A and at least 2 points in Row B. Must explain the logical connection between evidence and the thesis.

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt (Row D — Alternate Perspective): Responds to an opposing perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal. Must describe an alternate perspective AND engage with it.

Examples that earn the point: 'While checks and balances provide institutional stability, they can be slow to respond and may entrench the interests of powerful groups — social movements provide a more immediate and direct mechanism for representing citizens who are underrepresented by formal institutions.' 'Social movements can effectively pressure the government, but without institutional mechanisms to codify those changes, their impact may be temporary — constitutional checks and balances create lasting structural protections for the people's will.'
parts.scoring_notes: Must have earned Row A. Must describe an alternate perspective AND refute, concede, or rebut it.
